key: brown|1987|amazon https www amazon com po
bare: - Brown & Levinson (1987). — [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Politeness-Universals-Language-Interactional-Sociolinguistics/dp/0521313554)

- **Brown & Levinson's politeness theory** (1987) from pragmatics — specifically the analysis of face-threatening acts, which refusal invariably is. This is the linguistic literature on how refusal is actually performed, and it is [WELL-ESTABLISHED] as theory, though not tested as an intervention.
- **Brown & Levinson (1987)** incorporated hedging into their **politeness theory**, positioning hedges as tools for managing face in social interaction.

Brown and Levinson's (1987) influential politeness theory is built on a Gricean foundation: politeness strategies are analyzed as systematic departures from Gricean maxims, motivated by the need to manage "face" (the public self-image). Indirect requests ("Could you possibly pass the salt?") are seen as face-saving deviations from the maxim of Manner (be brief, be direct).
- Brown & Levinson (1987). *Politeness.* — [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Politeness-Universals-Language-Interactional-Sociolinguistics/dp/0521313554)

- Brown, P. & Levinson, S. (1987). *Politeness: Some Universals in Language Usage*. Cambridge University Press. [The face-based theory of politeness that underwrites Category 3.] [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Politeness-Universals-Language-Interactional-Sociolinguistics/dp/0521313554)
Brown & Levinson's (1987) politeness theory defines "face" as the public self-image that every person wants to claim for themselves, consisting of two components:
Brown and Levinson's (1987) politeness theory is arguably the most influential framework in pragmatics. It is also one of the most criticized. The critiques cluster around several major themes:

Brown and Levinson's (1987) politeness theory (see Category 3) explains why many fallacies are so difficult to counter: **correcting a fallacy is a face-threatening act**, and the social cost of this face threat often exceeds the perceived benefit of logical accuracy.
Brown and Levinson (1987) took Goffman's unitary concept and split it into two complementary needs that every person has simultaneously:
Brown and Levinson's Politeness Theory (1987) was developed partly to address this gap, explaining how face-threatening acts motivate systematic departures from Gricean norms.

**Camm-Crosbie et al. (2019)** in their qualitative study titled "'People like me don't get support'" documented autistic adults' experiences of mental health services and found a consistent pattern of misdiagnosis, inappropriate treatment, and feeling unsupported. The phrase the title captures — "people like me don't get support" — is an accurate empirical summary of the broader pattern.

bare: - **Cazalis et al. Chapman and Carel (2022) extend this analysis, arguing that autistic thriving has been "rendered both invisible and unthinkable by interlocking forms of testimonial and hermeneutical injustice." They identify an epistemic catch-22: **one can be recognized as autistic or as thriving, but not both** -- because mainstream conceptions of the good life have excluded autistic manifestations of happiness and flourishing.
- Chapman, R. & Carel, H. (2022). Neurodiversity, epistemic injustice, and the good human life. *Journal of Social Philosophy*, 53(4), 614-631. [Wiley](https://doi.org/10.1111/josp.12456)

- Clark, H. H., & Brennan, S. E. (1991). Grounding in communication. In L. B. Resnick, J. M. Levine, & S. D. Teasley (Eds.), *Perspectives on Socially Shared Cognition* (pp. 127-149). APA. [Stanford PDF](https://web.stanford.edu/~clark/1990s/Clark,%20H.H.%20_%20Brennan,%20S.E.%20_Grounding%20in%20communication_%201991.pdf)

- Clark, H.H. & Brennan, S.E. (1991). "Grounding in Communication." [Stanford PDF](https://web.stanford.edu/~clark/1990s/Clark,%20H.H.%20_%20Brennan,%20S.E.%20_Grounding%20in%20communication_%201991.pdf)
- Clark, H. H. (1996). *Using Language*. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press. [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Using-Language-Herbert-H-Clark/dp/0521567459)
- Clark, H. H. (1996). *Using Language*. Cambridge University Press. [Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Using-Language-Herbert-H-Clark/dp/0521567459)
